about_you_the_client.what_are_your_strengths_skills_talents_a:
I would say one of my main strengths is that I’m reliable and steady under pressure. In my current job as a warehouse supervisor, I’m responsible for coordinating a small team on the late shift, making sure orders are picked and packed correctly and safely. I’ve developed good organisational skills – I like breaking big tasks down into smaller steps and making simple checklists so nothing is missed. My manager often comments that I’m the one he can count on to stay calm when things get hectic, like when a lorry is delayed or a big last-minute order comes in.

I’m fairly practical and hands-on. I’m good with tools and basic repairs – at home I’ve fitted shelves, fixed small plumbing issues, and assembled furniture for friends and family. I also pick up new systems pretty quickly. When our workplace changed to a new stock management software last year, I volunteered to try it out first and then helped show the rest of the team how to use it.

In terms of personal strengths, I try to be a good listener. Friends often come to me when they’re going through something, especially other dads I know. I don’t always know what to say, but I try to listen properly and not judge. I’ve been through periods of stress and low mood myself, so I’m more aware now of how important it is to talk about things instead of bottling them up.

My main goals and aspirations at the moment are:
- To get my mental health to a more stable place so I can be more present and patient with my son. I notice I can get snappy when I’m overwhelmed, and I really want to break that pattern.
- To build my confidence around talking about emotions and asking for help. I grew up in a family where men didn’t really talk about feelings, and I’m trying to unlearn that.
- To progress my career in a way that feels sustainable. I’m interested in moving into a team leader or logistics coordinator role in the next couple of years, possibly doing some formal training in leadership or health and safety.
- Longer term, I’d like to be in a position where I can support other men who are struggling – maybe through peer support, mentoring, or volunteering. I don’t feel ready yet, but I’d like to learn from groups like JourneyMEN how to do that in a healthy way.

Overall, I see myself as someone who wants to do the right thing, provide for my family, and keep learning how to be a better dad, partner, and person. I’m hoping being part of this service will help me build on my strengths and work on some of the areas where I’m stuck.
about_you_the_client.what_are_your_interests_hobbies:
Outside of work, my biggest interest is anything to do with football. I play five-a-side with a group of mates most Wednesday evenings when I can make it, and I follow Wolverhampton Wanderers quite closely. I try to get to a couple of matches a season, usually with my younger brother. Football is a big way I blow off steam and stay connected with friends.

I also enjoy running, although I’m not as consistent as I’d like to be. I’ve done a couple of 10k runs in the past for charity and would like to work up to a half marathon at some point. When my son is with me at weekends, we go to the local park and kick a ball around or ride scooters together. I’m trying to make exercise more of a regular habit because I’ve noticed it helps my mood a lot.

At home, I like cooking simple, hearty meals – things like stir fries, pasta bakes, and Sunday roasts. I follow a few cooking channels on YouTube and occasionally try new recipes. I also enjoy listening to podcasts, especially ones about mental health, parenting, and personal development. Recently I’ve listened to a few episodes about men’s mental health and it’s made me realise I’m not alone in feeling the way I do.

I’m into music as well – mostly indie rock, 90s/00s stuff, and a bit of hip-hop. I often have music on in the background when I’m tidying up or driving. Sometimes I’ll put together playlists for different moods – more chilled stuff for evenings and more upbeat tracks for when I’m trying to motivate myself to go for a run.

I wouldn’t call it a hobby exactly, but I’ve started journalling a bit in the evenings – just writing down what happened in the day, what I’m worried about, and a couple of things I’m grateful for. It’s something a counsellor once suggested and I’ve found that when I actually do it, it helps clear my head before bed.

I’m also interested in learning more about DIY and basic home improvement. I watch a lot of how-to videos and have slowly been building up a small toolbox. Long term I’d like to be confident enough to tackle slightly bigger projects, like putting down laminate flooring or doing up a garden shed.
about_you_the_client.are_you_a_carer_or_have_carers_responsib:
Yes. I’m a separated father and have joint parental responsibility for my 7-year-old son, Oliver. He lives primarily with his mum, but I have him every other weekend from Friday after school to Sunday evening, plus one evening during the week where I pick him up from school, do dinner, homework, and bedtime routine before taking him back.

Although he doesn’t have any diagnosed disabilities, he has been described by his school as having some emotional and behavioural difficulties, especially around transitions and changes in routine. This can make things quite intense at times, and I’m working hard to learn how to support him better when he has meltdowns or gets very anxious.

On top of that, I keep in close contact with the school and attend parents’ evenings, meetings with his teacher, and any special reviews they set up. I often coordinate with his mum about school events, medical appointments, and holiday arrangements. It sometimes feels like a lot to juggle emotionally as well as practically.

I also provide informal support to my mum, who is 68 and lives about 20 minutes away. She has arthritis and some mobility issues. I visit her most Sundays when I don’t have my son, take her shopping, help with heavy lifting, and do small jobs around the house like changing light bulbs, mowing the lawn, and dealing with bills or online forms she finds confusing. She is still mostly independent, but I am the main family member nearby, so I’m the one she relies on most if something goes wrong.

All of this means that I often feel pulled in different directions – wanting to be a good dad, help my mum, and still keep on top of work and my own mental health. Part of why I’m seeking support from JourneyMEN is to learn how to balance these responsibilities without burning out or feeling constantly guilty that I’m not doing enough.
about_you_the_client.how_did_you_hear_about_journeymen:
I first heard about JourneyMEN through my GP practice. I went in a few months ago because I’d been struggling with low mood, trouble sleeping, and feeling overwhelmed with co-parenting and work stress. During the appointment, my GP mentioned that there were local community organisations that focused specifically on men’s mental health and peer support.

He gave me a leaflet with a few different services listed, and JourneyMEN was one of them. A couple of weeks later, I was talking to a colleague at work during a break, and he mentioned that he had actually attended some JourneyMEN groups himself after his divorce and found them really helpful. Hearing about his experience made it feel more approachable and less intimidating.

After that, I looked up JourneyMEN online, read through the website, and watched a short video where some of the men shared their stories. I recognised a lot of what they were talking about – feeling pressure to be “strong” all the time, not wanting to burden family or friends, and bottling things up. That’s when I decided to make a referral and see if this could be a good fit for me.
